text: The blackout struck without warning on a Friday evening, plunging the entire district into darkness.
At first there was chaos — car horns, shouts, the sound of things falling.
But within an hour, something unexpected happened.
Neighbours who had never spoken began to interact.
Someone lit candles on their front steps; another dragged a barbecue onto the pavement and started cooking for anyone who was hungry.
Children ran through the streets, thrilled by the novelty of a world without screens.
An elderly man told stories on his porch while teenagers listened, genuinely fascinated.
The atmosphere was intense yet warm, a peculiar blend of emergency and celebration.
Power was restored by midnight, and the neighbourhood promptly returned to its routine.
But for those few hours, the community had witnessed what life looks like when distractions vanish — and many felt a keen longing to preserve that feeling somehow.
